skill.name: telegram-automation
skill.description: Automate Telegram bot workflows through Rube MCP, including messages, media, chat details, invite links, updates, and bot commands.
skill.summary: Automate Telegram bot messaging, media sharing, chat management, updates, and command setup through Rube MCP.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static command-execution, weak-cryptography, and system-reconnaissance alerts are false positives caused by Markdown inline code, Telegram parameter names, and documented tool names. The skill still carries medium risk because it instructs agents to use an external MCP endpoint and Telegram tools that can send, delete, forward, and retrieve messages if authorized.

medium_findings.title: Authorized Telegram Actions Can Affect Real Chats
medium_findings.description: The skill documents workflows for sending messages, media, edits, deletes, forwards, updates, chat history, invite links, and bot commands. These actions are legitimate for a Telegram automation skill, but they can expose or modify chat content if used with broad bot permissions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented tool sequences clearly include state-changing Telegram operations and message retrieval. The context supports legitimate automation, so the concern is permission and data exposure rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: External MCP Network Dependency
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to add the Rube MCP endpoint and use it for Telegram operations. This is expected for the skill, but it means Telegram activity depends on an external service connection.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The MCP endpoint and Composio Telegram toolkit are explicitly documented. No hidden endpoint or covert exfiltration pattern was found in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Tool Names Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command alerts point to Markdown inline code and fenced examples that name Rube and Telegram tools. I did not find Ruby backtick execution or host shell commands in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged syntax is Markdown formatting around tool names, parameters, and examples. There is no executable Ruby, shell script, or command substitution context.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Keyword Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts occur in ordinary description, chat_id, commands, and character-limit text. I did not find hashing, encryption, or cryptographic API usage in these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain Telegram workflow prose and parameter names only. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic implementation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Telegram Metadata Misread as System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts refer to Telegram chat IDs, updates, chat history, and quick-reference tool names. I did not find host system inventory, process listing, network probing, or local environment discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines describe Telegram resource identifiers and Telegram API helpers. The context does not show reconnaissance against the user device or infrastructure.

dangerous_patterns.title: Networked Messaging Automation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill enables an agent to operate Telegram through an external MCP service. Users should review requested actions before sending, deleting, forwarding, exporting links, or reading messages.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is documented and intentional for this skill. Risk depends on granted Telegram bot permissions and user confirmation practices.

content.value_statement: Telegram bot workflows can be hard to run correctly because each action needs the right tool, chat ID, and permission. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Rube MCP tool discovery and safe Telegram task execution.
actual_capabilities: Guides setup for a Telegram connection through Rube MCP.
Explains how to send text messages, photos, and documents.
Documents chat lookup, administrator lists, member counts, and invite link export.
Covers editing, deleting, forwarding, updates, and chat history workflows.
Shows how to set bot commands and answer callback queries.
Lists Telegram limits for messages, captions, files, and rate controls.
limitations: Requires an active Rube MCP connection and Telegram bot authorization.
Cannot bypass Telegram bot permissions or chat membership requirements.
Does not provide executable scripts or a standalone Telegram client.
Tool schemas may change, so users must search available tools before running actions.

use_cases.title: Send Team Notifications
use_cases.description: Post release notes, alerts, or status updates to a Telegram group with the correct chat ID and formatting mode.

use_cases.title: Manage Community Channels
use_cases.description: Review chat details, export invite links, update command menus, and send channel announcements through a bot.

use_cases.title: Build Bot Support Flows
use_cases.description: Retrieve recent updates, answer callback queries, and forward selected messages for support triage.

prompt_templates.title: Verify Bot Connection
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether my Rube MCP Telegram connection is active. Search for the current Telegram tools first, then verify the bot identity without sending any messages.

prompt_templates.title: Send a Formatted Message
prompt_templates.prompt: Send this message to the Telegram chat I provide. Confirm the target chat first, use HTML formatting when supported, and warn me before sending.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Channel Update
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Telegram channel update with a photo and caption. Check caption length, confirm the channel permissions, and send only after I approve the final text.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Bot Workflow Permissions
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the Telegram workflow I want to run. Identify required bot permissions, rate-limit risks, message privacy concerns, and a safe sequence of Rube MCP tool calls.

output_examples.input: Send a release announcement to our Telegram channel.
output_examples.output: The assistant verifies the bot, checks channel access, prepares the formatted announcement, asks for approval, then sends it through the Telegram message tool.

output_examples.input: Find the chat ID for a group and set bot commands.
output_examples.output: The assistant retrieves chat details, confirms the group identity, prepares the full command menu, and explains that setting commands replaces the current list.

output_examples.input: Forward recent support messages to an internal triage chat.
output_examples.output: The assistant gets recent updates, avoids duplicate processing with an offset, confirms the destination chat, and forwards only the selected messages.

best_practices: Search Rube MCP tools before every workflow so parameter schemas are current.
Confirm chat IDs, destination names, and message content before taking state-changing actions.
Use the least privileged bot permissions needed for each chat or channel.
anti_patterns: Do not send messages, delete content, or export invite links without user confirmation.
Do not assume a username resolves to the same chat without checking chat details.
Do not process updates repeatedly without tracking offsets or duplicate messages.

faq.question: Does this skill include a Telegram bot token?
faq.answer: No. Users must configure their own Telegram bot through Rube MCP and BotFather.

faq.question: Can the bot send messages to any Telegram chat?
faq.answer: No. The bot must be added to the chat, and some actions require administrator permissions.

faq.question: Why should tools be searched before each workflow?
faq.answer: Rube MCP tool schemas can change. Searching first helps the assistant use current parameters.

faq.question: Can this skill read private messages?
faq.answer: Only messages available to the authorized bot can be retrieved through Telegram tools.

faq.question: Does the skill run local shell commands?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The reviewed file uses Markdown backticks for tool names and parameters.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: The main concern is authorized network access to Telegram chats, messages, media, and invite links.
